quote: I got the bill the electricity bill of men for $700.
stewart_read: Guadalupe volunteers her $700 bill unprompted. This is the bill anchor arriving as a gift — the rep did not have to extract it. The schema requires capture AND emotional flip (alignment-against-utility). The rep acknowledged with 'I don't blame you' but did not flip the figure into a value bridge. Capture happened; the flip did not.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: rep was holding the flip for after the orphaned_flow sequence resolved the existing-solar question. Rejected — the rep never ran orphaned_flow, so no later flip was set up.
coaching_implication: Show the rep the schema's capture-and-flip pattern. The $700 figure was live on the table. A suggested rephrase (no fabricated numbers): 'Seven hundred dollars — and that's with solar already on the roof. That's the problem we need to solve. Let me ask you a couple of quick questions about that system so I know exactly what we're dealing with.' This bridges the bill pain directly into the orphaned_flow diagnostic instead of treating them as separate conversations.

quote: Yeah. And another another month, it was $580. Another month was $5.20. Another 5 was $4.80.
stewart_read: Guadalupe delivers three additional months of bill data in rapid succession — $580, $520, $480 — building a multi-month pattern of high bills despite having solar. This is the flip opportunity: four months of data, all elevated, all with solar supposedly running. The rep's response ('I don't blame you') is empathetic but drops the data entirely. The figures are never named back, never totaled, never used as leverage.
coaching_implication: This is the primary flip opportunity in the call. The rep had four real numbers from the customer's own mouth. A suggested rephrase (using only Guadalupe's stated figures): 'So you're looking at $700, $580, $520, $480 — with solar on the roof. That's the system not doing its job. That's exactly what our specialist looks at. Let me ask you a couple of quick questions.' The flip doesn't require fabricated savings projections — the customer's own bill sequence is the indictment.

quote: So it's ridiculous. That's too much money for electricity. And I called medical. They said, you know, that's what it is.
stewart_read: Guadalupe has already tried to get help from her utility and was dismissed. This is a direct alignment-against-utility bridge point — the utility told her to accept the bill. The rep's response ('Yeah.') acknowledges but does not use this moment. The utility dismissal is the emotional proof that the existing system isn't working and that the utility won't fix it. It is the strongest setup for a value bridge in the call.
coaching_implication: The utility dismissal is a gift. A suggested rephrase: 'They told you that's just what it is — with solar on the roof. That's not okay. That's exactly why people call us.' This takes the utility's own words and turns them into the reason for the appointment. The rep walked past it entirely.

quote: Gotcha. Okay. Yeah. I would definitely just check with him. The issue is is we won't be able to do anything on equipment that might not be our own.
stewart_read: The rep applies Ion's third-party-equipment policy before running the orphaned_flow diagnostic sequence. The policy is real — Ion cannot service equipment it did not install. But the sequence exists to determine whether the policy applies: own vs. lease, company name, system size, working status. None of these questions were asked. The rep is not wrong about the policy; the rep is wrong about the sequence. This is premature policy application, not policy ignorance.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: rep recognized a likely DQ and chose to exit cleanly rather than waste time on a dead lead. Rejected — the orphaned_flow sequence takes under two minutes and could have revealed either (a) Ion installed the system (immediate callback path) or (b) a hard DQ with a roofing or new-system cross-sell opportunity. Exiting without the sequence forfeits both.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, walk the rep through the orphaned_flow questions in order. The coaching frame is not 'you applied the wrong policy' — it is 'you applied the right policy at the wrong step.' The sequence determines whether the policy applies. Skipping the sequence means the rep is DQ'ing leads that may be Ion's own customers.

stewart_read: Guadalupe interrupts to take another call and asks for ten seconds. The rep responds with 'Yeah. You're okay.' — calm, no pressure, no filler. When Guadalupe returns 40 seconds later, the conversation resumes without friction. This is a small but clean moment of composure under interruption.
coaching_implication: Note this positively in the 1-on-1. The rep held the space without crowding the customer. The interruption did not destabilize the call — Guadalupe came back and re-engaged. This is the kind of low-key composure the schema's energy_match principle describes.

quote: Somebody called me from another company talking about roof. I don't need no roof. I had a roof.
stewart_read: Guadalupe explicitly closes off the roofing cross-sell path — she has a roof and does not need one. This is a negative cross-sell signal. The rep does not acknowledge it, but it is important for the handoff brief: roofing is not a viable cross-sell angle for this customer. The schema's roofing bridge template does not apply here.
coaching_implication: No coaching action needed on the rep's handling of this moment — they did not push roofing. The value is in the handoff brief: if this lead is ever re-engaged, do not open with a roofing angle. Note it for the manager as a reminder that cross-sell signal detection includes negative signals that close off paths, not just positive ones that open them.

quote: Okay. I'm a save the number, and I'll call you back when I have my husband answer.
stewart_read: The husband surfaces for the first time in the final seconds of the call — as a reason Guadalupe will call back, not as a co-decision-maker to include in a booked appointment. The rep does not acknowledge the husband's role, does not attempt to lock a time, and does not apply the spouse-inclusion protocol. The call ends with a soft, unstructured callback promise. Per the schema, the correct move is to include the spouse in a locked appointment, not to accept 'I'll call back when he's available' as a close.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the call was already winding down and the rep judged that pushing for a locked time would feel aggressive given the unresolved existing-solar question. Partially valid — but the rep could have said 'When do you think you and your husband would both have a few minutes? I can call you back at a specific time so you're not chasing me.' That locks a time without pressure.
coaching_implication: Walk the rep through protocols.spouse_decision. The husband mention at 04:53 is not a throwaway — it is the first indication of a co-decision-maker. Even in a call that is winding down, the rep should attempt to lock a callback time with both parties. A soft 'I'll call back' with no date is a near-certain no-show.

quote: Definitely give us a callback if it is our system, and then we can get you helped out. But, yeah, in the meantime, I'll I'll take you off the list so we're not on the bottom of your calls.
stewart_read: The rep proactively removes Guadalupe from the call list before confirming any DQ criteria. This is the most consequential single action in the call: it converts a live, emotionally engaged lead with unresolved qualification status into a cold inbound callback that may never come. The rep's intent is considerate (not bothering the customer), but the effect is to surrender the lead entirely.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: rep is managing the customer's experience by reducing contact friction, which could improve callback likelihood. Rejected — removing from the list without a locked callback time or confirmed DQ means Ion loses the ability to follow up. The customer's parting line ('I'll save the number') is not a commitment.
coaching_implication: This is the moment the manager should replay in the 1-on-1. The rep had a customer who was still asking for help ('what do you recommend me to do?') and chose to remove her from the list rather than run the two-minute diagnostic that would have determined next steps. The coaching frame: 'Taking someone off the list is a DQ action. DQ requires a completed diagnostic. What diagnostic did you run before you made that call?'
